title: GDPR Processor Personal Data Breach Notification SLA

compliance.assurance_rationale: GDPR Art.28(3)(f) — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.28(3)(f) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.29: GDPR Processor Personal Data Breach Notification SLA.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.1.29' running on index itsm and supporting catalogue-defined data sources, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: GDPR Art.33 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that GDPR Art.33 (Breach notification to supervisory authority) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.1.29: GDPR Processor Personal Data Breach Notification SLA.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.1.29' running on index itsm and supporting catalogue-defined data sources, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: `index=itsm` (vendor security incident emails parsed to tickets), `index=vendor_sec` (HEC from processor portals), `gdpr_processor_register.csv`
app: Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928), HTTP Event Collector (HEC)
spl:
index=itsm sourcetype="snow:incident" category="*Vendor*" OR short_description="*processor*breach*" earliest=-30d
| lookup gdpr_processor_register.csv vendor_name OUTPUT notification_sla_hours
| eval reported=strptime(opened_at,"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| eval acknowledged=strptime(work_start,"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| eval ack_hours=round((acknowledged-reported)/3600,2)
| eval sla_hours=coalesce(notification_sla_hours, 24)
| eval sla_breach=if(ack_hours > sla_hours, 1, 0)
| where sla_breach=1 OR isnull(acknowledged)
| table number, vendor_name, opened_at, work_start, ack_hours, sla_hours, sla_breach
description: The processor must notify the controller without undue delay after becoming aware of a personal data breach. This use case measures time from processor-reported incident timestamps to internal acknowledgement tickets — ensuring contractual SLA (often 24–72 hours) is met and evidence exists for supervisory authority files.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for GDPR compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes itsm, vendor_sec and sourcetypes snow:incident. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Standardise processor breach intake in ServiceNow with vendor name and reported time; (2) store per-processor SLA hours in `gdpr_processor_register.csv`; (3) alert legal/DPO on approaching SLA; (4) integrate HEC if processors send structured webhook notifications; (5) retain closed tickets as Art. 28(3)(f) evidence. Indexes required: `index=itsm`, `index=vendor_sec`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=snow:incident`.
visualization: Table (SLA breaches), Single value (mean acknowledgement hours), Timeline (processor incidents), Bar chart (breaches by vendor). Key fields: `ack_hours`, `acknowledged`, `number`, `opened_at`, `reported`, `sla_breach`.

grandmaExplanation: We watch for the processor must notify the controller without undue delay after becoming aware of a personal data breach. this use case measures time from processor-reported incident timestamps to internal ackn. This is critical because gaps here could mean serious regulatory exposure or security blind spots.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928), HTTP Event Collector (HEC).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=itsm` (vendor security incident emails parsed to tickets), `index=vendor_sec` (HEC from processor portals), `gdpr_processor_register.csv`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Standardise processor breach intake in ServiceNow with vendor name and reported time; (2) store per-processor SLA hours in `gdpr_processor_register.csv`; (3) alert legal/DPO on approaching SLA; (4) integrate HEC if processors send structured webhook notifications; (5) retain closed tickets as Art. 28(3)(f) evidence.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=itsm sourcetype="snow:incident" category="*Vendor*" OR short_description="*processor*breach*" earliest=-30d
| lookup gdpr_processor_register.csv vendor_name OUTPUT notification_sla_hours
| eval reported=strptime(opened_at,"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| eval acknowledged=strptime(work_start,"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
| eval ack_hours=round((acknowledged-reported)/3600,2)
| eval sla_hours=coalesce(notification_sla_hours, 24)
| eval sla_breach=if(ack_hours > sla_hours, 1, 0)
| where sla_breach=1 OR isnull(acknowledged)
| table number, vendor_name, opened_at, work_start, ack_hours, sla_hours, sla_breach
```

Understanding this SPL

**GDPR Processor Personal Data Breach Notification SLA (Art. 28(3)(f), Art. 33)** — The processor must notify the controller without undue delay after becoming aware of a personal data breach. This use case measures time from processor-reported incident timestamps to internal acknowledgement tickets — ensuring contractual SLA (often 24–72 hours) is met and evidence exists for supervisory authority files.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=itsm` (vendor security incident emails parsed to tickets), `index=vendor_sec` (HEC from processor portals), `gdpr_processor_register.csv`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928), HTTP Event Collector (HEC). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: itsm; **sourcetype**: snow:incident. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=itsm, sourcetype="snow:incident", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• `eval` defines or adjusts **reported** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **acknowledged** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **ack_hours** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **sla_hours** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **sla_breach** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• Filters the current rows with `where sla_breach=1 OR isnull(acknowledged)` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Ticket_Management.All_Ticket_Management by All_Ticket_Management.status, All_Ticket_Management.priority, All_Ticket_Management.category | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**GDPR Processor Personal Data Breach Notification SLA (Art. 28(3)(f), Art. 33)** — The processor must notify the controller without undue delay after becoming aware of a personal data breach. This use case measures time from processor-reported incident timestamps to internal acknowledgement tickets — ensuring contractual SLA (often 24–72 hours) is met and evidence exists for supervisory authority files.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=itsm` (vendor security incident emails parsed to tickets), `index=vendor_sec` (HEC from processor portals), `gdpr_processor_register.csv`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928), HTTP Event Collector (HEC). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Ticket_Management.All_Ticket_Management` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.

Output fields: ack_hours, acknowledged, number, opened_at, reported, sla_breach, sla_hours, vendor_name.

Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (SLA breaches), Single value (mean acknowledgement hours), Timeline (processor incidents), Bar chart (breaches by vendor).
